metadata.name: VHF Nutrition Individual Types — Instance Data
metadata.description: Individual type instances for VHF Nutrition platform. Primarily B2C consumers with one B2B2C intermediary (Dietitian). Standalone individuals — no org hierarchy for B2C. Cross-refs: CRT-ONT customer roles, VP-ONT stakeholders.

examples.name: Fitness Enthusiast
examples.description: Passionate fitness consumer who actively researches and experiments with nutrition products to optimise training, recovery, and body composition. Regular gym-goer, CrossFit, or endurance sport participant. Early adopter of new formulations. Influential within their fitness peer group. Standalone individual — no org affiliation.
examples.motivations: Performance optimisation, body composition improvement, training recovery, community status, staying ahead of nutrition science, brand loyalty where quality proven
examples.painSummary: Information overload from conflicting nutrition advice, supplement quality uncertainty, budget vs premium ingredient trade-off, finding products that actually deliver measurable results

examples.value: Actively tracks macros or follows structured nutrition plan

examples.seasonality: January spike (New Year), September (return to routine), pre-competition peaks
examples.regulatoryContext: EFSA health claims regulation, Novel Foods, Informed Sport certification
examples.industrySpecificTraits: Brand-loyal once trust established. Values third-party testing. Follows fitness influencers. Shares product reviews in gym communities.

examples.influencers: Fitness influencers, gym peers, YouTube reviewers, Reddit r/supplements, personal trainer
examples.trustSignals: Third-party batch testing (Informed Sport), transparent ingredient lists, clinical dosing, peer reviews
examples.channelPreference: D2C website, Amazon, gym shop, subscription box

examples.name: Nutrition-Conscious Consumer
examples.description: Health-aware everyday consumer making deliberate food and supplement choices for general wellbeing, energy, and long-term health. Not a serious athlete. Reads labels, avoids ultra-processed foods, and seeks clean-label products. Purchases driven by trusted recommendations and ingredient transparency. Standalone individual.
examples.motivations: General health maintenance, sustained daily energy, immune support, clean/natural ingredients, value for money, simple supplementation routine
examples.painSummary: Confusion over which supplements actually work, distrust of marketing claims, too many product choices, unclear dosage guidance, subscription fatigue

examples.value: Actively reads nutrition labels and makes deliberate supplement choices
1 supplement product purchased in past 3 months

examples.seasonality: January wellness spike, autumn immune-prep, minimal summer dip
examples.regulatoryContext: EFSA health claims, food supplement regulations
examples.industrySpecificTraits: Influenced by GP/pharmacist recommendations. Values simplicity. Distrusts hype. Clean-label is a must. Supermarket availability is a plus.

examples.influencers: GP/pharmacist, friends/family, clean-eating blogs, newspaper health sections, Holland & Barrett staff
examples.trustSignals: Clean label, brand heritage, NHS Choices mention, transparent sourcing, no proprietary blends

examples.name: Competitive Athlete
examples.description: Performance-driven individual using nutrition professionally as a training tool — competitive amateurs, semi-pros, and elite amateurs across endurance, strength, team, and aesthetic sports. Nutrition is systematic, periodised to training cycles, and often guided by a coach or sports nutritionist. Highest priority: efficacy, purity (doping-tested), and precision. Standalone individual.
examples.motivations: Competitive performance gains, training recovery, periodised body composition, anti-doping compliance, evidence-based formulations, sports-specific macro/micronutrient targeting
examples.painSummary: Doping risk from contaminated supplements, finding batch-tested products, periodisation complexity, cost of multiple sport-specific products, navigating conflicting sports nutrition science

examples.value: Competes in organised sport at competitive amateur level or above
Follows periodised nutrition plan aligned to training phases

examples.seasonality: Pre-season peaks, competition season steady, off-season maintenance
examples.regulatoryContext: WADA anti-doping code, Informed Sport/Informed Choice, EFSA, Batch testing
examples.industrySpecificTraits: Non-negotiable on batch-testing certification. Periodisation-aware. Often coached. Values clinical dosing over marketing. Demands certificate of analysis.

examples.influencers: Coach, sports nutritionist, team-mates, Informed Sport database, PubMed, athlete forums
examples.trustSignals: Informed Sport certification, certificate of analysis, team endorsement, clinical trials, peer athlete use
examples.channelPreference: D2C website, specialist sports nutrition retailer, coach recommendation

examples.name: Registered Dietitian
examples.description: Registered nutrition professional who advises clients on dietary plans and supplement protocols. B2B2C intermediary — their recommendations directly shape purchasing decisions of multiple downstream consumers. Quality of evidence and professional credibility are paramount. May receive professional pricing or sampling programmes.
examples.motivations: Clinical credibility of recommended products, evidence base, regulatory compliance, professional reputation protection, client outcomes, CPD content from brands
examples.painSummary: Time-consuming product vetting, keeping up with new formulations, navigating EFSA vs marketing claims, client affordability vs clinical ideal, professional liability concerns

examples.typicalBudgetRange: N/A (client pays; may receive professional samples/pricing)
examples.regulatoryContext: HCPC Code of Conduct, BDA guidelines, EFSA health claims, Advertising Standards Authority
examples.industrySpecificTraits: Evidence-first. Distrusts marketing claims. Values peer-reviewed references. Professional samples programme is a strong channel. CPD content builds relationship.

examples.decisionTimeframe: Ongoing (continuous assessment of product portfolio for clients)
examples.influencers: BDA guidelines, EFSA database, peer dietitians, CPD conferences, PubMed, brand professional teams
examples.trustSignals: Peer-reviewed evidence, professional body endorsement, transparent formulations, HCPC-aligned marketing, brand professional support team
examples.channelPreference: Professional programme, CPD events, BDA conference, brand rep visit

examples.name: Parent / Carer
examples.description: Adult purchasing nutrition products primarily for family members — children, elderly relatives, or dependants with specific dietary needs. Safety-first, ingredient transparency, and age-appropriateness drive every decision. Highly price-sensitive across a household budget. Standalone individual.
examples.motivations: Family nutritional wellbeing, safety and ingredient transparency for children/dependants, age-appropriate formulations, household budget management, trusted brand credentials, convenient formats
examples.painSummary: Overwhelmed by product choice, unclear age-suitability guidance, conflicting advice on children's supplements, balancing quality vs household budget, sugar content in children's vitamins

examples.value: Primary purchaser of food/health products for at least one dependant
Places ingredient safety and age-suitability above performance claims

examples.regulatoryContext: Food supplements directive, EFSA children's health claims restrictions, sugar/sweetener guidance
examples.industrySpecificTraits: Brand trust paramount. Paediatric endorsement valued. Sugar-free/low-sugar critical for children's products. Supermarket visibility essential.

examples.influencers: GP, health visitor, school nurse, mum friends, Mumsnet/parenting forums, supermarket promotions
examples.trustSignals: NHS-aligned messaging, paediatric suitability label, sugar-free credentials, familiar brand, pharmacist recommendation

examples.name: Health Investor / Biohacker
examples.description: High-spending consumer treating personal health and longevity as a long-term investment. Embraces biohacking, quantified-self, longevity research, and premium supplementation stacks. Tracks biomarkers via blood testing or wearables. Motivated by measurable outcomes. Standalone individual.
examples.motivations: Longevity extension, healthspan optimisation, measurable biomarker improvement, cutting-edge evidence-based formulations, personalisation based on individual data, premium exclusivity
examples.painSummary: Information asymmetry on longevity claims, high cost of comprehensive stacks, supplement-drug interaction concerns, keeping up with rapidly evolving research, distinguishing evidence from hype

examples.value: Actively tracks biomarkers via blood tests, wearables, or quantified-self platforms
Primary health motivation is longevity/healthspan rather than athletic performance or general wellness

examples.typicalBudgetRange: GBP 150-500+/month on supplements and health services
examples.regulatoryContext: EFSA, Novel Foods (for cutting-edge ingredients like NMN, NAD+), MHRA borderline products
examples.industrySpecificTraits: Early adopter. Data-driven purchasing (blood test results trigger stack changes). Premium price tolerance. Values clinical trials, COA, and personalised recommendations.

examples.influencers: Andrew Huberman, Peter Attia, longevity podcasts, PubMed, blood test providers (Thriva, Medichecks), biohacking communities
examples.trustSignals: Clinical trials, certificate of analysis, bioavailability data, peer longevity community reviews, personalised recommendation engines
examples.channelPreference: Specialist D2C brands, subscription services, longevity clinics, biohacking conferences
